extra.reasoning:
The variable `rash` appears in only one rule: `interaction_drop` (priority 100, the highest priority in the system), which sets acuity := self_care (the lowest acuity level) when severe_headache, palpitations, back_pain, slurred_speech, and rash are ALL true. Because rash appears nowhere else, toggling rash true can only ever newly activate this drop-to-self_care rule.

Take the witness base input with rash=FALSE: {severe_headache=T, palpitations=T, back_pain=T, slurred_speech=T, rash=F, vision_loss=F}.
- With rash=false, interaction_drop does NOT match (it requires rash=true). Matching rules: raise_palpitations (p3→emergent), raise_slurred_speech (p3→emergent), raise_back_pain (p2→urgent), raise_severe_headache (p2→urgent). Highest priority = 3 → acuity = EMERGENT.

Now flip rash to TRUE, every other input unchanged: {severe_headache=T, palpitations=T, back_pain=T, slurred_speech=T, rash=T, vision_loss=F}.
- Now interaction_drop (priority 100) matches since all five required conditions are true. Priority 100 strictly exceeds every competing rule (max otherwise is 3), so it wins unambiguously → acuity = SELF_CARE.

Result: setting rash=true changes acuity from EMERGENT down to SELF_CARE. In the listed enum order self_care < routine < urgent < emergent, this is a strict decrease, directly violating the monotonicity property "setting rash true never lowers acuity." The witness_assignment records the BASE (rash=false) input; flipping only rash to true produces the lower acuity.
